doom.driver: Trump tells Congress ceasefire means he does not need their approval for Iran war. The president writes that hostilities "have terminated" because of the ceasefire, arguing he does not need congressional authorisation.
doom.blurb: PRESIDENT INVENTS OWN WAR POWERS DOCTRINE
doom.summary: Trump has informed Congress that the Iran war is over (it isn't — thirteen people died in Lebanon today and Israel is still striking), therefore he doesn't need their permission to keep fighting it. Meanwhile the Pentagon is quietly signing classified AI deals with OpenAI, Google, and Nvidia while pulling 5,000 troops out of Germany, suggesting the executive branch is consolidating wartime authority at the exact moment oversight mechanisms are being waved away. The ceasefire is doing a lot of heavy lifting for a situation where oil is still elevated and China's UN envoy says Hormuz dominates the Xi-Trump agenda.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Iran war continues despite ceasefire claims — Israeli strikes kill 13 in Lebanon, Trump rejects Tehran's latest peace proposal, and China flags Hormuz reopening as an urgent Trump-Xi priority.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Trump unilaterally declares the War Powers clock stopped by invoking a ceasefire his own officials are not treating as real, effectively sidelining congressional war authorization.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Pentagon strikes classified AI deals with OpenAI, Google, and Nvidia; NSA tests Anthropic's Mythos for vulnerabilities; NHS rushes to hide software specifically because AI hacking models like Mythos are deemed a credible threat.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Musk v. Altman trial reveals xAI distills OpenAI models and Musk warns AI could kill everyone — useful courtroom drama, but no new capability threshold crossed today.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: An alleged assassination attempt on Trump at the White House Correspondents' Dinner, Spirit Airlines collapsing amid jet fuel doubling, and Amazon AWS still offline in the Middle East from drone strikes paint a backdrop of cascading second-order war effects.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, interior scene looking outward — we are deep inside a wood-paneled executive corridor of a grand government building, the view framed by a tall arched doorway whose heavy mahogany doors hang open, dark crimson curtains pulled aside to reveal the outside world beyond. The interior is oppressive and shadow-heavy: dark walnut paneling absorbs the light, a grandfather clock on the near wall shows a number prominently on its face, and on a side table lies a sheet of cream-colored formal stationery bearing a gold circular seal at its top, dense black text filling the page, the numerals 60 visible in bold — the document catching the only warm interior light, a fireplace glow reflected in the polished floor. Through the arched doorway the outside world opens into a split panorama — on the left half, a Rose Garden in spring bloom, deep red roses backlit by a late Friday afternoon sun that sits low and blood-orange on the horizon, casting long charcoal shadows across white stone paths. On the right half, as if the world beyond bends impossibly, the Strait of Hormuz stretches in harsh flat light — a narrow dark waterway between tan arid cliffs, a massive low-riding supertanker silhouetted against the glittering strait, a sleek gray destroyer visible further back, its radar mast a dark spike against the smoldering orange sky. The entire scene is color-graded in dark crimson and charcoal, firelight reflections shimmering on every polished surface, high contrast, no text, no antialiasing.

dawn.driver: First malaria drug for babies is approved in 'major public health milestone'. WHO prequalification of Coartem Baby means newborns can be safely treated rather than using medication for older children
dawn.blurb: THE YOUNGEST LIVES JUST GOT SAFER
dawn.summary: The WHO has prequalified Coartem Baby — the first malaria treatment formulated specifically for newborns — a disease that kills hundreds of thousands of children annually, most of them in sub-Saharan Africa. In a world where the headlines are dominated by war, oil shocks, and rising hunger, quiet medical milestones like this one represent the stubborn, grinding work of human progress that never stops. Babies who previously had no safe treatment option now have one — and that is a straightforward, uncomplicated victory.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: WHO prequalification of Coartem Baby is a regulatory milestone that clears the path for global distribution to the most vulnerable patients imaginable.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: WHO prequalification requires coordinated international scientific review, signaling that global health institutions continue to function even amid geopolitical fracture.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Australia's progress toward eliminating cervical cancer — with zero new diagnoses in women under 25 — shows a second quiet medical triumph running parallel today.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Iran peace proposals reaching mediators in Pakistan kept oil prices from a new spike today, easing pressure on food and logistics systems the global poor depend on.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Nepal's restoration of a stolen 13th-century Buddha statue from New York is a small but real win for cultural justice and international cooperation.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Wide aerial view looking down at 45-degree angle, vast West African landscape dominating the frame. Methodist Hospital Ankaase Ghana seen from above, whitewashed rooftops glowing rich gold in afternoon equatorial sun, surrounded by dense saturated emerald canopy of tropical trees casting deep jade shadows across red laterite paths. The hospital compound tiny against the overwhelming lush greenery, louvered windows visible as thin amber lines on sun-warmed walls. Within an open ward courtyard visible from above, minuscule figures: a young mother in vivid kente-printed fabric of gold and green seated on a clinic bed, cradling a tiny infant, a white-coated doctor beside her, nurses moving purposefully across tiled floors. A spoon catching afternoon light like a jewel, holding a dissolving cherry-red tablet in luminous white breast milk, a single brilliant point of saturated crimson against warm honey-toned tiles. The scale overwhelms — this vast continent of emerald canopy and golden earth dwarfing the tiny room where something immense is quietly happening. Afternoon sun filtering through tropical leaves throws dappled amber-gold patterns across rooftops. Rich gold and vibrant emerald palette throughout, saturated and alive, light the color of honey pouring through jalousie windows.
